14.display: P(6) estimate = 3/5; too small a sample to trust because 5 rolls is not enough to reduce random variation.

17.display: The 500-pupil survey — a larger sample generally gives a more reliable estimate.

18.display: No — 20 out of 10,000 is a very small proportion (0.2%) and unlikely to reliably represent the whole batch.

19.display: Survey more people (increase the sample size).

20.display: It tends to get closer to the theoretical probability.

21.display: The 2000-spin result — larger samples give more reliable estimates.

22.display: A single roll only tells you what happened once — you need many repeats to estimate how often an outcome occurs on average.

23.display: With only 4 flips, getting 4 heads is easily possible by chance for a fair coin, since the sample is too small to detect bias.

24.display: No — 5 batteries is far too small a sample compared with 1000 to give a reliable estimate.

25.display: No — a bigger sample gives a more reliable estimate, but it is still an estimate, not necessarily the exact true probability.

26.display: The student with 2000 flips — a much larger sample is likely to give a relative frequency closer to the true probability.

27.display: Never — increasing the sample size generally makes an estimate more reliable, not less.

28.display: 8 spins is far too small a sample — the estimate 0.75 could easily be very different from the true probability due to random variation.

29.display: A sample of just 2 people is far too small and unlikely to represent the views of the whole country.

30.display: False — how large a sample needs to be depends on the situation (e.g. how much variation there is and how accurate the estimate needs to be), not a fixed number.

31.display: Accuracy improves gradually rather than in direct proportion to sample size, but larger samples still reduce the effect of random variation and give more trustworthy estimates overall.

32.display: 300 pupils across the whole school — this is a larger and more representative sample than just one class of 15.

33.display: 12 rolls is a small sample, so random variation could easily cause a relative frequency of 1/3 even with a fair dice; rolling the dice many more times (e.g. hundreds of times) would give a more reliable test.

34.display: P(enjoy) = 3200/4000 = 0.8; this is likely reliable because 4000 is a large sample size.

35.display: Researcher B's estimate is more likely to be close to the true proportion, since a sample of 3000 is much larger and more reliable than a sample of 30.

36.display: Estimated faulty items = 2000 (2/50 x 50,000); limitation: a sample of 50 out of 50,000 is very small, so the estimate may not be accurate.

37.display: Larger samples give more reliable estimates of how the whole population will vote, reducing the effect of random variation from asking only a few people.

38.display: P(red) = 750/3000 = 1/4; this is likely a good estimate because 3000 is a large sample size.

39.display: A design using a large number of drops (e.g. 100 or more), calculating the estimated probability as the number of "point up" landings divided by the total number of drops.

40.display: For example, flipping a fair coin just 3 times could give 3 heads (relative frequency 1.0), far from the true probability of 0.5, purely by chance, because the sample is so small.

41.display: A larger sample reduces the effect of random variation, giving an estimate that is more likely to fall within a small margin of the true value; a rough estimate can tolerate more variation, so it can use a smaller sample.

42.display: With 10 flips, the relative frequency of heads could easily be quite far from 0.5 (e.g. 0.7); with 100 flips, it would likely be closer to 0.5; with 10,000 flips, it would almost certainly be very close to 0.5, showing that reliability improves as sample size increases.

45.display: Sam (45 out of 50) — a much larger sample gives a more reliable shooting rate than 9 out of 10.

46.display: No — 6 items from 12,000 is far too small to claim the whole batch is perfect.

47.display: Always (usually) — with more fair coin flips, relative frequency of heads generally moves closer to 0.5.

48.display: Eight rolls is a small sample — random variation can give relative frequency 1/4 even on a fair dice.

49.display: No — 3 pupils is far too small to claim 100% of the school walks.

50.display: The 500 flips — a much larger sample gives a more stable estimate of P(heads).

51.display: Only 10 games is a tiny sample — the win rate can change a lot as many more games are recorded.

52.display: The estimate moved from 0.7 toward 0.5 as the sample grew — larger samples usually give more reliable estimates.

53.display: False — two surveys of the same size can give different estimates because of random variation.

54.display: Yes — many years of daily data usually give an estimate closer to the long-run chance of rain.

55.display: True — even very large samples give estimates that may still differ slightly from the exact true probability.

56.display: Lab B (20 in 500) — testing 500 batteries gives a more reliable failure rate than testing 5.

57.display: Sloan (10,000 rolls) — the much larger sample is likely to give a relative frequency closer to 1/6.

58.display: 200 attempts over a season average out luck better than 12 attempts in one afternoon.

59.display: No — opening only 2 boxes is far too small a sample compared with thousands of boxes.

61.display: Relative frequency usually settles nearer the true probability as trials keep increasing.

62.display: Never — even huge samples give estimates, not guaranteed exact equality with theoretical probability.

63.display: Not very surprising — 40 flips is still a small sample where 0.55 can happen by luck on a fair coin.

64.display: The result after 10,000 flips (0.501) is usually closer to true 0.5 than after only 50 flips.

65.display: Not exactly — larger samples reduce luck's effect but estimates still need not match the true probability exactly.

66.display: Your friend with 2500 spins — the much larger sample is likely nearer true 0.25.

67.display: Always — a small sample can swing farther from the truth than a large sample from the same experiment.

68.display: 20 users is too small — the percentage could change a lot when many more people watch.

69.display: The group with 1500 drops — many more trials give a better estimate of P(point up).

70.display: Less — 1400 pupils should give a steadier rate with less bouncing around than 14 pupils.

71.display: 600 rolls is a large sample, so random variation is small and relative frequency can be very close to 0.5.

72.display: False — relative frequency can still change slightly as more trials are added.

73.display: Only 4 trials — far too small to estimate P(both heads) as 0.5 for fair coins.

74.display: The shop testing 800 seeds — a much larger sample gives a more reliable sprouting rate.

75.display: Thousands of voters spread across areas give a more reliable picture of national voting than 20 people at one station.

76.display: It means the relative frequency stops swinging wildly and stays near the true probability.

77.display: Sometimes — 1000 trials usually help but do not guarantee being within 0.01 every time.

78.display: Your sister's 3000 flips — a huge sample shows long-run fair coin behaviour better than 3 flips.

79.display: 2000 chests give a much more stable estimate of the true drop rate than 20 chests.

81.display: 40 faulty in 1000 tests — overall fault rate 0.04 (4%).

82.display: A whole month of buses is a larger sample, giving a more reliable on-time rate than one morning.

83.display: False — a larger sample is usually closer but not guaranteed to be closer every time.

84.display: Class 2 (52 heads in 100 flips) — 100 flips give a more reliable estimate than 9 flips.

85.display: As sample size grows, the estimate usually gets closer to the true probability (e.g. nearer 0.5 for odd on a fair dice).

86.display: One extra trial barely changes a tiny sample — you need many more trials to improve reliability.

87.display: No — 12 spins is too small to call blue the luckiest colour.

88.display: Always — pooling trials increases total sample size and usually improves the estimate.

89.display: The national study of 5000 patients — much larger and wider sample.

90.display: No — both samples contribute; the larger combined sample refines the estimate rather than making the first useless.

91.display: Season-long averages use many more attempts, so they are steadier than one-match stats.

92.display: Monday's 7 items alone — such a tiny sample is weak for estimating the true defect rate.

93.display: Never — two different runs of 500 and 5000 trials will not necessarily give identical relative frequencies.

94.display: Sample size of one day and treating one trip as the whole story — need many days for a reliable rain estimate.

95.display: Site Y with 2000 reviews — far larger sample than 5 reviews.

96.display: The estimate from 400 rolls (0.158) — larger sample, closer to true 1/6 than 40 rolls.

97.display: False — bigger samples reduce uncertainty but estimates still need not equal the exact true probability.

98.display: 300 pooled flips — combining many pupils' flips gives a much larger sample than one pupil's 10 flips.

99.display: 600 players: estimate 420/600 = 0.7 — much more reliable than 6/6 = 1 from only 6 friends.

100.display: The estimate stops changing much and stays near the true probability as more results are collected.

101.display: Weak — only 2 flips can give 0.5 by luck; far too small to prove fairness.

102.display: Less — 2500 adults should give a steadier percentage with less jumping around than 25.

103.display: Always — more flips make it easier to spot bias because relative frequency has less room to hide it.

104.display: Adding 900 flips pulled the overall relative frequency closer to 0.5 (0.499).

105.display: No — 15 donors is too small to treat 4/15 as exact truth for budgeting.

106.display: Factory Q from 4000 items — much larger sample than 40 items from Factory P.

107.display: False — 15 trials can be far from theoretical probability by luck even with fair equipment.

108.display: Example: deciding a coin is biased from 3 flips — collect many more flips to get a reliable estimate.

109.display: Group B (0.167 from 360 rolls) — larger sample, likely closer to 1/6 than Group A.

111.display: Doubling trials improves reliability but error does not usually halve exactly.

112.display: Never — even 2000 fair coin flips give an estimate, not guaranteed exact equality with 0.5.

113.display: 20 viewers is tiny — completion rate could shift a lot with thousands of viewers.

114.display: 700 kicks over a year — much larger sample than 7 kicks in practice.

115.display: Early wiggles come from small samples; flattening shows the estimate stabilising as trials increase.

116.display: False — random variation means two runs of the same size can give different relative frequencies.

117.display: Record many more lunch breaks (larger sample) across different weeks.

118.display: 180 wins in 300 games — same 60% but 300 trials give a more reliable long-run estimate than 30.

119.display: Long-run experiment (5000 spins) gives estimate 0.199 much nearer true 0.2 than 50 spins.

120.display: Always — for a fair experiment, more trials usually move relative frequency toward theoretical probability.

121.display: Eight people at one rally is far too small — report sample size with any percentage.

122.display: The student with only 25 drops should do many more trials — 2500 is already a strong sample.

123.display: Estimate 3/100 = 0.03 winners; limitation — only 100 tickets scanned from 50,000.

124.display: False — the next 500 trials can still shift the overall relative frequency, though usually less than early trials.

125.display: Many repeats average out luck so one odd run is not trusted alone.

126.display: Seven heads in 10 flips can happen by luck — need many more flips to judge fairness.

128.display: Never — samples of 50 and 500 will not necessarily give the same relative frequency.

129.display: 1 million opens is a huge sample — far more trustworthy than 50 test opens.

130.display: Poll 2 with 10,000 people — larger sample more likely near national result.

131.display: 100 pupils (0.72) — larger sample likely nearer true 0.75 than 10 pupils at 0.9.

132.display: False — a nice-looking fraction from very few trials can still be unreliable.

133.display: First 30 spins can vary a lot by luck even when true probability is 0.25.

134.display: Estimate from 600 trials is data-based; true probability is the long-run value the estimate approaches.

135.display: 496 ups in 1000 drops — much larger sample made the estimate more reliable than 4 in 11.

136.display: Never — larger samples help a lot but some random variation remains.

137.display: 0.4 from 1000 trials — regulators trust larger samples more than 10 trials.

138.display: Too few flips; relative frequency 0.5 can occur by luck — need many trials and it remains an estimate.

139.display: 50 successes in 100 trials — pooled relative frequency 0.5.

140.display: False — two small runs can both be unlucky; one continuous larger experiment is usually better.

141.display: 600 minutes over a month — much larger sample than 6 minutes one rush hour.

142.display: Early trials swing with luck; after thousands of trials relative frequency is usually nearer the final long-run value.

143.display: Sometimes — relative frequency 0 in a small sample can happen by luck even if the event is possible long-run.

144.display: No — 6 plays is too small to disprove a 1 in 3 claim.

145.display: Coin B (398/800 ≈ 0.4975) looks more like a fair coin than Coin A — larger sample near 0.5.

146.display: 1200 passengers (70%) — airline should use large survey, not 7 friends on one train.

147.display: Always — each new trial can change the relative frequency (though changes shrink as sample grows).

148.display: Describe wiggly early results, closer near 100 flips, very steady near 1000 for a fair coin.

149.display: Testing 120 items per hour smooths random jumps seen when only 12 items are tested.

150.display: False — the same estimate from a tiny sample is usually less reliable than from a huge sample.

151.display: Not always as good — pooling is fine if both use fair methods, but 10 flips in a row under same conditions is similar to 10 total.

152.display: Sloan (88 sixes in 540 rolls) — much larger sample gives stronger evidence of possible bias.

153.display: Always — more fair random trials usually move relative frequency toward theoretical probability.

154.display: Early 55% from 20 visits was unreliable — 51% after 20,000 visits is a much more trustworthy estimate.

155.display: Rare events need many more trials to see stable relative frequency than common 50–50 events.

156.display: False — 'big enough' depends on how rare the event is and how accurate you must be.

157.display: Class Y learned more — 2400 spins with estimate 0.251 near true 0.25 beats 24 spins at 0.33.

158.display: 50 heads in 100 flips — pooled relative frequency 0.5.

159.display: Sometimes — a larger sample is usually closer but can occasionally be farther by bad luck.

160.display: Flip at least 20–30 times (or more) with everyone watching — still quick but larger than 3 flips.

161.display: 500 match penalties (0.76) — much larger sample than 5 training kicks.

162.display: False — relative frequency still changes slightly as more trials are added after 1000.

163.display: Compare both sample sizes and relative frequencies — larger samples usually give more trustworthy estimates.

164.display: Many nets of 1200 fish — repeating large samples estimates the tagged fraction more reliably.

165.display: Never — small samples often give estimates that miss the true probability.

166.display: 15 opens is tiny — zero drops can happen by luck even at 10% true rate.

167.display: Survey B (900/1000 city-wide) — larger, more representative sample for a city plan.

168.display: False — all estimates are between 0 and 1 but tiny samples can still be very misleading.

169.display: Kid-friendly law of large numbers: flip a fair coin many times and heads fraction gets nearer half.

170.display: Stopping from 2/9 failures is overreacting — tiny sample compared with usual 2% from 9000 items.

171.display: Always — pooling many days increases total trials and usually improves the estimate.

172.display: 1800 flips at 0.502 — huge sample supports 'about half heads' better than 18 flips at 0.61.

173.display: Counter-example: large sample of dice rolls can give relative frequency 0.166… not a 'round' decimal.

174.display: False — two large samples can differ by 0.02 by chance; that does not prove they were too small.

175.display: Spin at least 200–400 times and divide wins by total spins to estimate P(win).

176.display: 8 of 9 patients is a tiny early trial — readers need sample size to judge reliability.

177.display: Never — relative frequency keeps updating, though changes get smaller as trials grow.

178.display: 6000 hours of logs — much larger sample than 6 hours for comparing crash rates.

179.display: 0.333 from 3 trials is one success only; 0.333 from 300 trials is 100 successes — far more meaningful.

180.display: False — friends may not represent everyone; random sampling matters as well as sample size.

181.display: Yes — 198/1200 = 0.165 is close to 1/6 ≈ 0.167, consistent with a fair dice at large sample.

182.display: 70 red in 280 spins — pooled relative frequency 0.25 (or 1/4).

183.display: Always — combining years pools data into a larger sample for a better estimate.

184.display: Graph flattening near 0.5 shows the estimate stabilising as sample size grows.

185.display: Check sample size first — 1400 flips is far more reliable than 14 flips even if percentages differ.

186.display: False — zero in 20 trials can happen by luck even if the event is possible.

187.display: Estimate 3/3 = 100% melted from tiny sample — test many more bars before rejecting 8000.

188.display: Always — for fair experiments, more trials usually move relative frequency toward the correct value.

189.display: Advantage: less random error; limitation: takes more time/cost and still may not hit exact truth.

190.display: Early 90% from 10 players was unreliable — 62% after 10,000 shows small samples mislead.

191.display: Always — pooling adds all trials to one total; Monday's 6 and Tuesday's 600 both count in the denominator.

192.display: 1600 trials give a much more reliable relative frequency for choosing the spinner.

193.display: False — large samples get close but need not match theoretical probability to the nearest whole percent every time.

194.display: Report that 2400 rolls give a more reliable test than 24 rolls when results disagree.

195.display: Adding flips moved relative frequency from 0.44 toward 0.498, showing long-run fairness with larger samples.

196.display: Always — more spins make bias easier to detect than a handful of spins.

197.display: Census of everyone — largest possible sample, more reliable for the whole country than 40 people.

198.display: False — longer runs help refine estimates even when early results looked fine.

199.display: Three bullets linking larger samples, relative frequency as estimate, and closeness to true probability.

200.display: 900 reports over two years — much larger sample supports a safer decision than 9 in a fortnight.
